sentences.text: The experiment.
First of all, they did sterilization.
And then they made different operation on woman, like taking out the woman business that just from sadistic way.
I found-- I remember the Dr.
Dering.
He was a Polish doctor, but a very sadistic man.
Imagine making those unnecessary operations for young women without anesthetic, without any medication.
It was terrible.
The girls cried and screamed, and he just did it.
There was a doctor, Dr.
Samuel, which was a very famous doctor.
He said that the sterilization, what Dr.
Clauberg is doing, they are not so dangerous, because Dr.
Clauberg stealed his ideas, because he did work on this idea, and he didn't finish it.
So even he, he sterilize the women, but they still will be able to have children.
Because as he didn't finish that, so that is not so dangerous.
More dangerous was the other operations what, what they did.
And Dr.
Samuel--
Was a Jewish doctor.
They came to the clinic.
And Dr.
Dering too, from the men's camp.
But Dr.
Samuel, he did reconstructing or healing if something was wrong.
He didn't want to do unnecessary like Dering did.
But he said that if I one day don't come back, that means they found out that I didn't do the operation what they wanted.
And that's what happened.
One day he didn't came back.
So we knew that either-- that Dering put him in or the SS man put him in.
They did such terrible operation that there was a French woman--
No.
No.
No.
French woman.
Adelaide Hautval was her name.
From what?
There was hundreds of women.
The, the women the experiment done was Jewish.
All of them.
They was Belgish.
A lot of them they been Greek and Belgish.
Some Polish, but mostly Belgian and Greek women.
All Jewish.
So-- and this woman, she was assigned to the experimental block.

But she-- from beginning she said, I am not going to do the operations which are not necessary, and not without anesthetic, and not without healing, because when I did my doctor diploma, I promised to do my utmost best to help.
So--
Adelaide Hautval.
She was from France.
She came with a transport with French political women who'd been in d'Arcy.
It was a prison, and from there, there came about 300 of them to Birkenau.
At one stage, I was assigned by Katka to be their blockalteste.
So we knew each other.
Experimental block.
I was.
I was sent by punishment there because they knew that is a closed up.
You can't go out.
It's limited.
Now, I-- I had to look after everything there.
I also-- from beginning, I assigned the people to the doctors, because someone said that Clauberg is not so dangerous.

So I'm-- where, where I could, I put as much patient as possible to Clauberg, because that wasn't so dangerous, it wasn't so painful, and like I said, it didn't have symptoms for the future in case they survived the war and can have children.
Arranged doctors, and about the food, the rations, because they needed bread and, and, and-- so this this is--
Beg pardon?
They went out.
They went out.
All those women, block plus the staff.
They I brought with me.
When they said I am going for punishment to-- sent to the new Barrack 10, so I said, but I need 16 girls.
[LAUGHS] always had my-- all right.
So I picked myself 16 girls who was working outside work, because here at least I knew that is inside work.
Train to Germany.
And when they been in the room--
Because there was a doctor had here-- there his laboratorium, or whatever, ever he did the experiments in this camp, in another camp.
No.
But the-- one woman who was working there, like a nurse, her name was Mila Portashinski, she was intelligent girl.
And he said, Magda, they-- how can they make-- find out that?
They must kill us first and open the brain, and then find out.
So it's not a sanatorium, what he promised.
It will be all finished at crematorium.
I knew that it won't be so easy, but I thought, I-- they know that they are going to death, and I just opened the door.
So their death or my death-- I let them out.
I take the risk.
When they arrived to the train, two was missing.
So that started the story.
So that happens.
They, they wanted to kill me or to hang me or to shoot me, because this is sabotage.
How dare I let the girls out?
And next to the Barrack 10 was Barrack 11, and this was where they hang or shoot or killed or whatever the [INAUDIBLE] or any enemies to the Germans.
And there as a hangman was a Jewish wrestler by the name [?
Jakov. ?] And he sent me a message that he will do it quick.
[LAUGHS]
I was thinking how, but going backwards, the Greek girls who was there with the experiments, they been so grateful because I helped where I could before the operation or after operation.
So they made me-- I don't know from-- how they got the material-- two beautiful pillows and they put it on my bed.
What do I do with two beautiful pillows?
So when Hauptsturmfuhrer Dr.
Wirths came in and he left the door open, and the car was there outside.
So I ran with the pillows and I threw it in-- in the car.
Was beautiful handmade pillows.
He didn't say then nothing.
When I came back from the commander alive, Dr.
Wirths was standing there waiting for me, and he says, thank you for the pillows.
So I knew that he did save me from being killed.
But the women, they heard that I went very easy out of it, so they sent Irma Grese--
Beg pardon?
They checked what happened with me.
Is she dead?
[LAUGHS]
The, the woman commander--
-- from Birkenau.
Mandel and Drexler.
So when they found out that I am alive, nothing happened to me, and I will be happily living after, so they sent Irma Grese to bring me to the Birkenau.
I ask her, Irma, tell me.
Where are you taking me?
And she said, you will see.
They took me to Birkenau, and under the earth there was a little-- like a room, so small that four people could stand next to each other.
There wasn't even room that you could sit or go down, because it was so tight that four women was next to each other.
In the morning, I had to stand roll call.
And after the roll call, the punishment was that I should wash all the toilets.
When I arrived, very tired, suddenly a group of men came.
I don't know who they are till now.
I don't know how they found out.
And they said, we heard what is your fate.
So you lie down, and we will wash all the toilets for you.
If somebody will come, one from us will watch, and then you quickly get up, and-- so that, that you are working.
Quickly I got up.
So I didn't know what to do.
I sat on the toilet.
[LAUGHS]
The Blockfuhrer who was friend of the other SS man, who told him that I did the painting this time for the good of the SS too.
So they been friends.
And when he heard that-- what happened to me-- so he had a certain with the other-- they been friends-- to, to save me from it.
Because seven days I couldn't manage.
There-- so many toilets, and all night standing, then the roll call, then again the roll call.
No, no.
I was-- I was seven days standing, and I was seven day doing toilets.
But not I did it-- the men did it.
And the last day, he arrived and took me straightaway.
[INAUDIBLE].
I did do the punishment.
It was-- it was not a block of-- like the Jewish women was.
It was a wooden barrack, because they was mostly non-Jewish women who worked in the offices and in the establishment.
Yeah, it was.
It was.
And they were used to it, you know, to do-- like when it was the counting, they did all the businesses, because they been the [NON-ENGLISH], you know-- the high society.
But when I came there, I said, look.
Here-- he brought me there.
And I said, when he went, I didn't ask to be your blockalteste.
But one thing I ask you.
I will do everything possible to help you, but you have to listen to me.
If I say roll call, then drop everything and stand on the roll call, because it's not right that because you are office girls or what people should stand hours because the counting is not good.
And also when I said that taking bread, then you should.
Taking the food, you should.
I can't do everything by myself, but I will do everything for you.
And I did.
The first day, you know, I went there where all the woods were, and I, I said that the SS man, Tauber, who took me to the barrack, he sent me here to exchange the blankets.
We had trolleys, which we took with my staff.
I had some-- about 16 girls who helped me.
And I want new blankets.
It's a order of his.
He didn't order me, but they didn't go to check.
And I-- and I came back, and we made the bed with the new blankets.
I don't know exactly which--
Let me see.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Let's say middle of '43.
Because I went--
It was nice.
It was nice.
It was, because I know that we were standing outside.
It was pleasant.
The number I don't know.
No.
They, they was there, but I wasn't there too long either, because some others arrived.
And then Katka put me there, you know, because she said you will be take care of them, if somebody will be there so he, he won't and you will save as much as you could.
Oh, what?
No.
I got assignment to another barrack.
Till some other people didn't arrive.
It was sometimes more weeks or less weeks.
Weeks.
Weeks.
[INAUDIBLE].
Weeks, and the Dutch women arrived.
And there I wasn't the blockalteste.
I was just the, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the top and the second.
Beg pardon?
No, no.
The blockalteste was there, and I was her [NON-ENGLISH].
Like, I represented her.
Or--
Deputy, yeah.
I was her deputy.
And I was-- because the Holland girls, they were such fine, soft-- like milk and honey--
Jewish, yes.
So I was always trying to, to help them and to tell them what and how to make life easier.
But they was-- they was not for life.
No.
They couldn't take it.
I saw.
You see, they came home and they couldn't take the hard work.
I don't say all, but some of them, like, they decided they don't want to live.
So they put a finger up, they looked up and like a statue, and they died.
And, and some of them-- because they selected them out quickly.
So they put them to a barrack, which was called 25, where they collected.
If they had enough, then they didn't want to go with every.
So they collected them.
And it was-- the barrack was full, and also the backyard was full of them.
And it was hot, hot, hot.
So-- and I saw them poor girls outside and their faces swollen, and they been thirsty.
So I came-- excuse me.
Have some-- a bit of-- have a bit of water.
Yeah.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] [DRINKS]
No.
They were Jewish girls.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But they were so tough working there, you know.
So it was so tough because they worked with it.
That's it.
After that, I went to work-- it was end of '33-- '43-- and I went to work to the office.
I said, I have enough.
I said--
Katka.
I said, I had enough.
I had all the nationality.
I had Russian.
I had-- I had one Russian village with children, babies, and, and that.
They was very good.
I was very good to them.
Because the commander, when he picked me, so he said, you are a kindergarten teacher, so you will be looking after them.
So I said, if I have to look after them, then I don't want them to be on the roll call.
I want them to be able to be going to the sauna to be washed and to bathe, and I want them to have SS food.
And I thought, no.
He's going to give me a, a flap.
And he said, you have-- you will have it all.
When the Russian girls found out-- Russian women-- went out, because the women from-- till 30, they been in a different barrack.
So they saw they are heaven, what I provided them.
So they carried me all around that would take you to [RUSSIAN].
But anyway, in the future they did repay me many times what I did for them.

Anyway, so I had the Russian, I had the Polish, I had a Czech, I had a French, I had the mix marriages with children, and then I had the barracks with-- with Dutch girls, the Belgium girls, what-- Polish girl.
All.
So I said--
No, no, no.
The, the Czech, the Polish, the Russian, they, they was all non-Jewish.
French, yeah, non-Jewish.
The only small group was the French, but the rest, there was thousands.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
Beg pardon?
Clerical worker.
